description: Explains how Craft is built from source, how the client and multiplayer server are launched, and how the Python server depends on the compiled world-generation library.
document:
Craft is intended to be built as a small native OpenGL game plus a separate Python multiplayer server. The README presents source setup as a dependency-first process: install CMake and the platform libraries, clone the repository, configure with CMake, build with make, then start the game executable. On macOS the documented prerequisite is CMake, with Homebrew shown as one way to install it. On Ubuntu the dependency command installs CMake, GLEW development files, X.org development headers, cURL development files, and GLFW build dependencies. On Windows the README expects CMake, MinGW on the PATH, and a cURL install whose library and include directories can be found under Program Files.

The native build is defined by `CMakeLists.txt`, which keeps the client build straightforward. It gathers the C files under the source directory, adds bundled third-party C sources for OpenGL extension loading, PNG loading, noise, SQLite, and threading, and builds a single executable named `craft`. The file also adds the bundled GLFW project, exposes include directories for the bundled dependencies, requires cURL from the host system, and links different system libraries depending on Apple, Unix, or MinGW builds. The Windows path has an extra search hint for cURL because the README expects a conventional Program Files installation. In normal Unix-like use, the documented build commands are to run CMake in the checkout, run make, and execute the resulting game binary.

Running the client has two basic modes from the setup perspective. Starting `./craft` with no extra arguments launches the game locally, while passing a host and optional port connects the client to a multiplayer server. The README also describes an in-game online command with the same host and optional port shape, and an offline command that selects a save file when the player wants local play. The default source-build workflow therefore produces the client only; multiplayer needs an additional server process if the old public server is not being used.

The self-hosted server has one important build step of its own. The README shows a GCC command that compiles the noise implementation and the client world-generation source into a shared object named `world`, using the source and noise include directories. This is not a second copy of the terrain algorithm in Python. Instead, `world.py` loads the compiled library from the current directory through ctypes, wraps the C world creation callback, and exposes a `World` object with a small chunk cache. That arrangement lets the Python server generate the same default terrain as the C client before applying player edits from the database.

The server entry point is `server.py`. Its startup code accepts an optional host and port, otherwise listening on all interfaces at the default Craft port, logs the server address, creates the model, starts the model thread, attaches that model to a threaded TCP server, and then serves forever. The model opens the SQLite database, creates tables for block edits, lights, signs, and optional history, commits periodically, and processes queued client events. The server can also be run with a cleanup argument, which prints SQL to remove stored block rows that match generated terrain or protected items rather than starting the TCP service.

A developer should treat the Python tools as server-side companions rather than as part of the CMake client build. The multiplayer server needs the compiled world library beside it, and `builder.py` is a script for programmatically placing blocks through a live server connection. It builds geometric sets of block coordinates, authenticates using a local identity database and the Craft web identity endpoint, then sends block messages to the server. For ordinary setup, build, and run questions, the key distinction is that CMake builds the native client, while Python runs the network server and related automation once the shared world-generation library exists.
